Indigenous Australians Civil Rights Movement← All Lessons
Lesson 3 of 4

The 1967 Referendum & Land Rights

💭 Thinking Question

Imagine you were born in Australia, grew up here your whole life — but the government said you weren't a citizen. You weren't counted in the population. Laws about you were made without your voice. How would that feel, and what would you do?

🎯 Learning Intention

By the end of this lesson I will be able to explain the significance of the 1967 Referendum, describe the ongoing fight for land rights including the Mabo decision and Aboriginal Embassy, and write a persuasive text arguing for the most effective method of advocacy used by First Nations peoples.

⚠️ Warning: This lesson contains historical images and film involving First Nations peoples, including images of deceased persons.

The 1967 Referendum

Study the 1967 Referendum information and campaign materials from your booklet:

📹 Watch
First Australians — The 1967 Referendum

The 1967 Referendum Questions from your booklet:

1967 Referendum Questions

1How long ago did Australians vote to change our country's laws to recognise Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Australians as citizens?
2What were Australians being asked to vote on in the 1967 Referendum?
3Before this Referendum, how were Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples considered as part of the population if they were not citizens?
4After the Referendum, how did life change for the First Nations population? List at least one positive and one negative impact.
5How do you think being left out of the Constitution affected Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples?
6Explain why the 1967 Referendum was important in Australia's human rights history.

Land Rights

Study the Land Rights images and documents from your booklet:

The Mabo Decision

Mabo Day

The Aboriginal Embassy

Land Rights Persuasive Text

The persuasive writing task and reminder about Pathos, Ethos and Logos from your booklet:

Land Rights Persuasive Text

Explain the ways First Nations People advocated for change and choose which you think was the most effective. Use evidence from all information studied. Write 1–2 paragraphs. Remember to use Pathos, Ethos and Logos.

🪞 Lesson Reflection

The 1967 Referendum achieved over 90% YES — one of the highest in Australian history. Why do you think so many Australians supported this change?
Eddie Mabo fought for 10 years and died before learning the outcome. What does this tell us about the commitment people have made to justice?
Looking at the timeline so far — White Australia Policy → Stolen Generations → Freedom Rides → 1967 Referendum → Mabo — what pattern do you notice about how change happens?
